title: Struct miscellany

exercises.title: Nested Struct Address Book
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Create nested structs to represent a person with an address, then print formatted contact information.

## Input
- Line 1: a person's name (single word, no spaces)
- Line 2: city name (single word, no spaces)
- Line 3: zip code (integer)
- Line 4: a second person's name (single word, no spaces)
- Line 5: city name (single word, no spaces)
- Line 6: zip code (integer)

## Output
Print exactly two lines, one per person, in this format:
```
<name>: <city> <zip>
```
Then print a third line with the total size in bytes of one `Person` struct, in this format:
```
sizeof(Person) = <size>
```

## Requirements
1. Define a struct named `Address` at namespace scope with exactly two members in this order: `std::string city`, `int zip`.
2. Define a struct named `Person` at namespace scope with exactly two members in this order: `std::string name`, `Address address`.
3. In `main`, read input and create two `Person` objects using aggregate initialization (brace initialization), including inner braces for the nested `Address`.
4. Print each person on their own line using dot notation (including chained dots for nested members): `<name>: <city> <zip>`.
5. Print the size of the `Person` struct using `sizeof(Person)` on the third line in the exact format shown above.
6. Use `std::cout` for all output.

## Notes
- The exact value of `sizeof(Person)` is platform-dependent. The test cases use the expected output from a typical 64-bit platform (g++ on Linux, as used by the judge). Trust the judge output.
- You may use `#include <iostream>` and `#include <string>`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- two people with different cities
Hidden -- same city for both people
Hidden -- short names and small zip codes

exercises.title: Sensor Report with Padding Discovery
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Build a sensor reporting system using a struct with mixed-size fundamental types. Print each reading and then reveal how struct padding makes the struct larger than the sum of its members.

## Input
- Line 1: number of readings `N` (1 <= N <= 5)
- Next `N` groups of 3 lines each:
  - Line 1: a single character grade (`A`, `B`, or `C`)
  - Line 2: a floating-point value (the sensor reading)
  - Line 3: an integer sensor ID

## Output
- `N` lines, one per reading, in this format:
  ```
  Sensor <id>: <value> [Grade <grade>]
  ```
  Print `<value>` with exactly one decimal place (use `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(1)`).
- Then one blank line followed by three lines:
  ```
  sizeof(char) + sizeof(double) + sizeof(int) = <sum>
  sizeof(Reading) = <actual>
  Padding bytes = <actual - sum>
  ```
  Compute all three values in code using `sizeof` expressions -- do not hardcode numbers.

## Requirements
1. The struct `Reading` is provided in the starter code with members `char grade`, `double value`, `int id` (in that order). Do not change it.
2. This member ordering is intentional -- it produces padding on 64-bit platforms because `double` requires 8-byte alignment but follows a 1-byte `char`.
3. In `main`, read `N`, then read each reading's data. Store readings in the provided `Reading data[5]` array. Use aggregate initialization (brace init) for each `Reading`.
4. Print each reading in the format shown above.
5. After the readings, print the blank line and the three size lines. Compute the member sum as `sizeof(char) + sizeof(double) + sizeof(int)`. Compute actual size as `sizeof(Reading)`. Compute padding as the difference.
6. Use `std::cout` with `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(1)` for the value output.

## Notes
- On the judge (64-bit Linux, g++), `sizeof(char)=1`, `sizeof(double)=8`, `sizeof(int)=4`, so the member sum is 13. The actual struct size is 24 due to alignment padding. Your code should compute these values, not hardcode them.
- You may use `#include <iostream>`, `#include <iomanip>`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- three readings with mixed grades
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Sensor 101: 98.6 [Grade A]
Sensor 202: 72.0 [Grade B]
Sensor 303: 55.3 [Grade C]

sizeof(char) + sizeof(double) + sizeof(int) = 13
sizeof(Reading) = 24
Padding bytes = 11

test_cases.label: Hidden -- single reading
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Sensor 1: 100.0 [Grade A]

sizeof(char) + sizeof(double) + sizeof(int) = 13
sizeof(Reading) = 24
Padding bytes = 11

test_cases.label: Hidden -- five readings all grade B
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Sensor 1: 10.5 [Grade B]
Sensor 2: 20.3 [Grade B]
Sensor 3: 30.7 [Grade B]
Sensor 4: 40.1 [Grade B]
Sensor 5: 50.9 [Grade B]

sizeof(char) + sizeof(double) + sizeof(int) = 13
sizeof(Reading) = 24
Padding bytes = 11
